title: Textura quadrata — the formal blackletter
sources: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906) — public domain; Gothic scripts chapter, textura quadrata analysis. Historic manuscript tradition: the Gutenberg Bible was set in Textura Quadrata typeface, drawn from this hand.
notes: Textura quadrata (Latin: woven square) is the most formal blackletter variant: every vertical stroke is diamond-topped and diamond-footed, producing a woven texture across the page. Distinct from Gothic textura lowercase (which is slightly less rigorous). Covers: the diamond hairline serifs (made with the full nib width held flat for the entry stroke, then the main stroke), the compressed body width, the consistent diamond rhythm at top and bottom of every minim, pen angle (~45°), letter spacing so hairlines nearly touch but do not, the capital alphabet (which uses more ornament than the lowercase). Compare with Fraktur and Batarde: Textura quadrata is the most vertical and compressed.
